comments.content:
[IMAGE:0](I'm feeling unimaginative this evening, so it's just going to be a plain old ordinary Magic Monday with a strange alchemical diagram for its graphic. Deal.)

Once again, it's technically Monday now -- past midnight Eastern time -- and here I am on Dreamwidth, so it's time for another Magic Monday.  Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next Monday!***

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

A question about color breathing as I grimly roll my boulder back up the hill that is developing an actual meditation practice:

When you breathe the color in through your solar plexus to fill your body, does it empty out of the rest of the world so that only your body is filled with the color, or are both your body and the area around you filled with the color at that point?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It doesn't empty out of the world. When you're doing color breathing, you imagine yourself in the midst of an infinite ocean of the color you're working with, and since it's an infinite ocean, you can fill your body with it without decreasing the ocean one bit.

By the way, yes, I know, meditation is hard work -- probably the hardest work you'll ever do. The payoff is commensurate.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you! That makes perfect sense, regarding the infinite ocean.

Meditation is indeed hard work, although I do not quite understand why I feel such disproportionate resistance at times! Still, I continue to chip away at it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most people face an enormous amount of resistance to establishing a meditation practice. I'm not sure how much of that is common to humans everywhere and at all times, and how much is a function of some of the specific dysfunctional habits of our time and culture. One of these days I may try to start a conversation among people from different cultures about their experiences with starting meditation, to try to get a broader perspective on it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Our culture of immediacy has a huge impact on this as well as the data point that most people map meditation to eastern practices only.

replies.title: Music too
replies.content: I am reminded of trying to get students to practice guitar or piano (or insert instrument here).  Most people want to be instantly good at playing a tune, never understanding it takes years of cumulative, daily work to play songs/pieces above the Three Blind Mice/Kumbaya grade.  They think it is easy because professional musicians make it look that way.  They also go towards it with an inflexible, unforgiving, Land of Perfect attitude, for instance, the mom who forces her nine year old to master a Chopin etude for show or the beginning singer who doesn't practice and doesn't care to learn how and then gets upset when they fail an audition.  I honestly think the only reason I haven't had much of an issue meditating every day is because I have already been through all the pitfalls and massive failures of concentration that go along with dedicating a huge part of one's life to music.   To Anonymous: yes, modern culture has made it worse.  They now expect to be perfect within the interval of time it takes a movie montage to play out.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I would be very interested in the results of that conversation, if you ever get around to it.

I was somewhat surprised, since I do other things, such as crocheting, hunting, and staring blankly while daydreaming, that require patient concentration and a certain amount of stillness, so I was not expecting my mind to react to being required to sit still and breathe for five minutes with the internal equivalent of screaming shrilly and trying to fling itself over the nearest cliff!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

Thanks again for providing this forum to ask questions. It is much appreciated.
I was wondering if Tarot cards can send an "Error" message. For example if I were to ask a specific question and the card I draw has nothing at all to do with the question could I interpret that as an indication that the question does not have an answer at this stage or that I should perhaps ask the question in a different way?
Perhaps more generally how applicable should one expect Tarot answers to be on specific questions?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This sort of thing varies drastically from person to person. In my experience, though, very often when the card you draw doesn't seem to make sense, it's because you're thinking of the question -- or interpreting the card -- in too narrow a way. Ask yourself: if this was an answer to the question, what would it mean?

comments.title: Children
comments.content:
I know this came up before, but regretfully I cannot recall any detailed precautions.
So my question is: when the child is 5 is it safe to:
-proceed daily with the routine, as usually recommended by you, of banishing ritual, followed by meditation and divination/ Tarot reading?
-if not safe together, which element or elements are safe?
-does it matter if the child is present in the same home at the time or does it help to pick a time when she's absent from the premisses?

Also I hope to start my work through your Learning Ritual Magic some day soon. Should I wait for my child to be of specific age?

I know these are so basic to you and I am trying to speed reed through lots of the recommended literature here; even the quick read of LRM makes me ache of the work ahead .. but I think since I started paying attention to some things that happen to me but I was usually left with no good explanation or resolution ( contact with dead family members incl. sounds and moving objects; patterns of daily events so pervasive that it would be impossible to call random; specific 'lucky' numbers; answers to prayers) it becomes plain and logical. So I want to learn about it and practice.

Thanks a lot
A.

replies.title: Re: Children
replies.content:
If your child's five you can certainly begin the kind of basic rhythm of practices I've discussed -- and since that's also what's taught in Learning Ritual Magic, you should be good to go. It's usually more convenient to do practices when your child is somewhere else, simply because it minimizes the risk of interruptions! Still, it's not a hard and fast rule.

These practices are basic to me, sure, but I still practice a banishing ritual, a meditation, and a divination every day. That's the foundation of magical training, and you don't stop needing the foundation just because you've built things on top of it...

comments.title: Emptiness versus the gods
comments.content:
Is the combination of inner emptiness and desire for deep, meaningful relationships a good enough reason to start actively contacting one or more deities?

I don't want the gods to replace my friends, or family, or the search for Mrs. Right. But I would like at least one relationship in my life to be always there, independent of outside circumstances. Also, expecting humans to fill the inner emptiness seems a bit too much to ask.

replies.title: Re: Emptiness versus the gods
replies.content: Possibly, yes, but you might want to ask yourself this: what are you prepared to give to such a relationship?

replies.title: Re: Emptiness versus the gods
replies.content:
Effort to embody the virtues of the chosen deities, daily prayer, weekly offerings.

I would prefer to avoid having arbitrary restrictions placed on me (e.g. not eating certain foods), proselytizing, and having my life turned upside down without benefit to myself.

replies.title: Re: Emptiness versus the gods
replies.content:
I probably forgot to include my question, so here goes.

Considering what I am willing and unwilling to give, and what I am expecting in return, would you recommend that I seek relationships with gods or not? If not, is there anything else that Western occultism can offer me?

I dont´t think modern materialist society has much to offer in this area, except for filling my life with so much stuff that I don’t have time to feel empty.

replies.title: Re: Emptiness versus the gods
replies.content: I recommend that you discuss that with the gods. Prayer is the usual method, and most people know how to do it. Talk to them about it; let them know what you have to offer, what you're unwilling to offer, and why...and then pay attention to unexpected coincidences in the days following, and also to any nonverbal response you may get.

comments.title: Magic to Eliminate Procrastination?
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

Thank you for hosting Magic Monday. I cannot find it now but I recall that last week somewhere in the comments you mentioned that because of your practice you do not have problems with procrastination. Would you be willing to talk about this more specifically?

Sincerely,

MILLICENTLY LURKING

replies.title: Re: Magic to Eliminate Procrastination?
replies.content: Sure. It wasn't a matter of doing specific magical rituals to ditch my habit of procrastination; it was simply that I wanted magic in my life so badly that I was willing to push through the habit and establish a daily magical practice...and that taught me how to push through the same habit in other contexts. Procrastination is just a habit; like all habits, it gets stronger when you do it, and weakens its grip when you do something else.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I noticed you occasionally do astrological readings for people. Can I trouble you to do one for me, as I have no idea what is going on with me. I was born on the 29th November 1980 between 7.35 - 7.40pm in Halifax, West Yorkshire. Does it make a difference if you were conceived in a radically different place to where you were born, as for me that was Colombia. Fair warning, you might not like what you see. I've already had a remote viewing instructor stop answering my emails after he checked me out.

Thanks.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You can do your own horoscope on any of a number of free websites -- the one I tend to recommend is www.astro.com. Casting and delineating a natal chart takes several hours of fairly hard work, and it's time I don't really have to spare just now, so the free site is probably your best bet.

I have no idea what your remote viewing instructor thought he saw. Your horoscope has nothing particularly dreadful in it: Sun in Sagittarius, Moon in Virgo, the last decan of Cancer rising, a decent elemental balance, enough squares and semisquares to give you some backbone, enough trines and sextiles to give you luck. If you'd like to learn more, there are plenty of websites and plenty of books that will walk you through the meaning of each of the planetary placements and aspects in your chart, and give you a fair idea of the hand you've been dealt in this life; alternatively. if you're willing to pay for the service, any competent professional astrologer can give you a thorough natal reading and walk you through what it all means.

No, it doesn't matter if you were conceived somewhere else. The moment that counts is the moment when you get an independent energy body, and that happens when you leave your mother's body at birth.

replies.title: independent energy body
replies.content:
"The moment that counts is the moment when you get an independent energy body, and that happens when you leave your mother's body at birth."

Hello, where could I find out more about this? Specifically, is the energy body of an unborn person slowly developing and slightly different from, though continuous, with that of their mother, which then separates at birth and continues to develop, or does the unborn person have no energy body of their own, and simply partake of her energy body until necessity (birth) makes the impetus for the creation of a new energy body then and there?

replies.title: Re: independent energy body
replies.content: The fetus has its own energy body the way your pancreas or your lungs have their own energy body; energetically, the fetus is an organ of the mother's body until birth. The birth process disconnects the infant's energy body from the mother's in just the same way it disconnects the infant's  oxygen supply from the mother's; what was a subordinate energy system crystallizes into a fully independent energy body, and takes on the imprint of the astrological factors active then and there.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: At some point you've mentioned every Druid organisation I've heard of except for the British Druid Order. What do you think of them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't know much about them, as I don't spend anything like as much time in Britain as I'd like!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Not a question, but something that might be of interest to your readers, and possibly even to you, although I know you're not a fan of some uses of electronics.

I created, essentially as a programming experiment, Geomantic. It will either generate a completely random chart, or accept four Mothers you have cast offline. You can fill in some basic information such as who it's by and for an what it's about. It will then give you a chart, which can be bookmarked and shared and printed, and you can add notes to the chart.

I'm still adding features. The 12 Houses chart just went live a little while ago, and while it's not very good, it's the best I can do with CSS and HTML right now (I based it on a chart I found online, rather than your prettier one, which I could not copy).

I'm currently working on adding The Way of Points, and then later I'll add Triplicities, before I start looking at anything as challenging as figuring out how to display the relationships in the astrological version.

It is, as the page says, still very much in alpha testing, but I'd love to have some people go and test it, especially anyone who know enough to spot errors, something none of my current testers do.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nice! There are several geomancy programs in circulation at this point, but there's certainly always room for more, and this one has a nice clean appearance. My first test chart showed no errors on the shield chart.

The house chart, on the other hand, came out the wrong way around -- in a geomantic house chart, the first house is in the notional east, on the left hand center of the square, and the houses proceed *counterclockwise* from there. Still, as you've noted, this is in alpha testing, and there's plenty of time for fixes.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you! That's what I get for trusting a random image on the internet, instead of going to a solid source. I'll fix that houses chart.

comments.title: Update
comments.content:
Mr. Greer:

A blah besets you? While these creatures have their functions, I will now do my part to broom them away from you with two bits of odd true news that a working mage like you will likely like the taste of.

1) A few weeks ago you kindly confirmed that a geomantic chart I cast did in fact show conditions were/are good for me to train to be Tolkien for Michigan. I did so and am doing so with Swiftness at my back as that chart advised. I have likewise accepted all help offered.

Since beginning, my corner of this cosmos of ours has done all but rain Hobbits in my backyard. I have acquired all those books of Middle Earth again plus more good ones and founded a book club to study Tolkien with my lady love and her mom. The following seeds for a good setting for myths have also sprouted from my wayward education: names, maps both wild and artificial, human populations, food webs, a not-too-shabby simple conlang, and last but not least, banners! :)

I'm an astrological ignoramus: were/are stars good for such deeds? All this happened in like three weeks. Swiftness ain't playin'! lol.

2) I recently made and distributed a one page homespun comic to both fight my hated enemy Chronic Wasting Disease (that approaches my herds as I type) and to honor your celebration of the commonplace.

I finished it on last Friday morning. That same morning I used my might in grundcraeft aka natural magic aka conjoined human living to turn that comic into a charm most Saturnal and mildly Mercurial. Bwah ha ha ha...

SEVEN MINUTES after I delivered that educational charm to my extroverted test subjects for distribution a bad wind storm in my county turned into a terrible wind storm and knocked power out for thousands (including my address)for over 50 hours. It blasted neighboring counties too!

lol. Winning! I am Saturn's Pet. Friends of Mordor: fear me!

My charm was/is well-received during its distribution in my 'hood. I even got a modest gift from some local hunters in addition to a useful boost in reputation!

Thanks so much for holding this fort. Frodo knows we need it... Donation in progress btw. I'm already semi-collapsed to avoid rushes so it is a little bit of a dance for me to get some bucks into a PayPal.

Saturn's Pet

replies.title: Re: Update
replies.content: Glad to hear things are going well. As for blahness, it's a passing thing, and it sits with me for a little while and then goes its way.

comments.title: Reincarnation & Personality
comments.content:
Hi Jmg

Happy to report The Druid Magic Handbook arrived in the mail last week. Looks like a solid year of initial work to me.

My question. In the Druid tradition what is the role of personality in reincarnation? Does the soul get a whole new personality to be navigated like the new body and inate abilities? Or does the soul bring some sort of core personality along with it?

I look at my children and wonder which parts are inherited from their parents and which came with the core being

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation & Personality
replies.content: What passes from life to life isn't personality so much as character, the basic framework on which personality is built. The individuality, the permanent part of the soul, takes on a new personality with each new incarnation, but it's always shaped by the experience of previous lives. (Astrologically, your rising sign in this life was your sun sign in your last life.) So your children have their own essential characters, which come with them from their previous lives, but the personality they construct around that over the course of their childhoods is new, and derives partly from heredity, partly from environment, and partly from the astrological forces in action at the moment of birth.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation & Personality
replies.content: What exactly is meant by character? I'm not sure what you mean talking about the difference between personality and character

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation & Personality
replies.content: Character is more general. Someone who has, let's say, an outgoing character will express that in various ways depending on the details of the personality.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(from Myriam)

JMG, thank you for the opportunity to ask questions about magic. I don't know anyone who can converse with me about it.

I would like to check my recent musings with you. You say we end up on different mountains depending on the path we take, and I'm wondering how that relates to which forces we worship as deities, the nested aspects of the deities, whether we in turn nest in particular aspects, and whether that path towards a specific deity continues on through reincarnations.

If the forces we worship as deities are all aspects of the One Source, and time and cultures change their aspects to us, I think of the deities as nested like Russian dolls all the way into the Source. If each of us feels pulled to worship certain aspects of those deities and we do practices that shape us towards those deities, would it be correct to think that we nest in a particular aspect of a deity, and end up on that “mountain” because our souls choose, are called there, or ARE manifestations of that force? I'm thinking ultimate destination as opposed to floundering around till we get our heads right. Or do we nest in many different deities? Or are our souls a unique aspect of the One, as unique as each of the forces that became gods and goddesses?

And would this pull towards specific aspects of the Source hold true over reincarnations and beyond, into gwynfydd? Or would different lives have pulled us towards different deities based on the personality of each reincarnation? And so nest in none specifically, or in all those we worshipped.

If I desire wisdom above all else, for example, as opposed to music, power, or enlightenment, does that desire come from a previous life or from my soul? (I ask this because since I was a very young girl, I desired wisdom above all else.) In other words, am I a very tiny manifestation of the force that is wisdom working my slow way to nesting in her as my place in the whole that is the All? As would everyone else towards their own ultimate place.

I would really like to know your thoughts on all this.

And as if that wasn't enough questions, I'm wondering if you have encountered the goddess Ariadne?

Thank you for your patience!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Different schools of occult philosophy understand these issues in different ways. My take, for what it's worth, is that each soul is a unique expression of the cosmos, destined to fulfill a unique function in the structure of the whole...but that's a very, very long way off for those of us who are still busy learning the lessons of human incarnation. Each of us relates primarily to one of the great noetic beings, the divine Intelligences Proclus discusses in his writings, but being what we are here and now, that relationship has to work through intermediaries -- the forms and manifestations through which they relate to us, which change with ages and cultures.

Each of us also, in the course of our incarnations, has to reach some sort of balance with the other great powers of the cosmos, and it's quite common for a soul to take an entire life, or several lives, working with some power that isn't its own source, for the sake of balance. And then there are also imbalances that come with the vagaries of our various incarnations, and those have to be worked out, often via relationships with other divine powers.

So your desire for wisdom may be a reflection of the ultimate destiny of your soul; it may be the working out of one of the other relationships that will fit you for your proper role in the heights of Gwynfydd, when you get there; it may also be that you spent a bunch of lives being very, very unwise, finally got a clue, and came to this life with the clear sense that getting wisdom is your number one goal this time around.

You can't necessarily find out which it is. All you can do is follow that thread and see where it takes you.

As for the goddess Ariadne, no, I haven't really worked with any of the Greek deities. I know enough people who've had powerful experiences of them, though, to know that they're real, and mighty.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: (Laughing) I would put my money on having been very, very unwise. Thank you!

comments.title: We all have flat days...
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

The alchemical diagram has defeated me! They're good. :-)!

Just riffing off the McMansion discussion, do you reckon that meme's can, or are being used in thaumaturgy? In explanation, sometimes I encounter folks who have a 'get big, or get out' meme stuck in their heads - and it is immovable, even when the evidence points in other directions.

Thanks for the Magic Monday, and I had a pretty flat week just gone due to illness. And now a 'cut-off low' is fast approaching from Antarctica and should arrive just before the weekend. I'm unsure that weather experience will be flat!

Cheers

replies.title: Re: We all have flat days...
replies.content: I'm not sure how much thaumaturgy is involved in memes, and how much of their effect is the drool-spattered stupidity that seems to seize so many people these days!

comments.title: Daily recollections
comments.content:
Hello!

I have been doing daily recollections (amongst other daily practices such as meditations) for several years now.
I have noticed that I get now a lot of flashbacks from my own life of happenings of 30 years ago or more. It does not bother me at all, but would it be related to my practices? If so, what else could I expect to resurface?

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Daily recollections
replies.content: Good question. Are the events from 30 or more years ago things you still haven't really processed?

replies.title: Re: Daily recollections
replies.content: Thanks for the response, yes I think so! A few regrets I have about things I should have done differently in the past! Nothing major or terrible. But nevertheless in the background.

replies.title: Re: Daily recollections
replies.content: You might want to try taking some time to journal on the events and the regrets you still have. The method that worked best for me was to have an imaginary dialogue on paper between myself and whatever inner voice wanted to speak; that helped me to get a lot of buried emotional business to the surface and resolve it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

In your replies to others and in formulations I've read elsewhere, I've noted an underlying formula for a basic working that I'd like to verify:

Take a square of paper (torn, not cut). Inscribe on that square the target/object of the working (e.g. the people of the United States) a number of times. Turn the square clockwise 90 degrees. Inscribe the intention of the working across the previous writings (e.g. may we live in peace) an equal number of times. Fold the square in some manner and place under a candle of an appropriate color (e.g. red for healing). Meditate on the intention while burning the candle for a regular period daily until the candle is completely consumed.

Questions:
1) Is this formulation essentially correct?
2) I have seen both 7 and 9 given as the number of times the writings are to be repeated. Is one better than the other?
3) Does is matter how the paper is folded beneath the candle (e.g. quarters)?
4) What does one do with the paper once the candle is completely consumed and the working is complete?

Many thanks!

--David, by the lake

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's a good basic framework. You can also expand on it in various ways: for example, the candle can be "dressed" (anointed) with oils compounded to reinforce the magical purpose, you can dust the paper with powder or put five small drops of a magical oil on it (one in each corner and one in the center), and so on.

Odd numbers are powerful in traditional conjure. 3, 5, 7, and 9 are all used; I find 7 and 9 best for the paper work.

The paper should be folded in quarters. If you're trying to bring something toward you, fold the paper toward you; if you're trying to send something away from you, fold it away from you. When it's done, if possible, bury the paper in the back yard to keep what you've brought, or in the front yard to see to it that what you've sent away never comes back.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That is interesting! I will give it a try too.

But what if I am doing this for someone other than myself? Does it change the folding directions? Or does towards always mean invoke and away mean banish?

Also what do I do if burying the paper is not a possibility? Do I store it somewhere else? When I was working with chaos magic sigils, the instructions were generally to burn the sigil after the working is done, unless the sigil was supposed to work continuously.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If you're doing it for another person, first of all, you should always be sure to have their explicit consent. Second, it's best if they do the writing and folding themselves -- that links them to the working and guarantees that they'll get the benefit of it. If for some reason that's not an option, do the work at a time when you know where the other person is, and fold it toward or away from where they are.

Burying it allows the energy to diffuse into the earth and cycle back to the natural reservoirs of magical energy, without disrupting the spell. If you burn the spell paper, you undo the spell. With a little ingenuity, you can surely find a place to bury it -- there's got to be a green belt, a vacant lot, or some other piece of ground where you can quietly slip out with a trowel, make an incision in the ground, slip the paper into it, and walk away.

comments.title: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
comments.content:
For the past two or three months I've been practicing banishing rituals, daily discursive meditation and occult study. It's a bit too easy for me to treat divination like an addiction so I've been putting it on the shelf for the time being, although I've been getting quite a bit more into astrology which I find more balanced.

In this time I've noticed that my consciousness has indeed changed quite a bit; most noticeably I no longer enjoy watching youtube videos or reading popular novels for relaxation. Both strike me as very unpleasant now, and I wonder how I could have ever liked them! Equally I am increasingly less needy in my social realtionships. Perhaps this is because I don't share many interests with my old friends. What I would love to chat about, astrology, tarot card symbolism, herbalism, literature etc is something that would baffle and frighten my friends. So passing time with others is increasingly seeming more like a chore than a pleasure.

With my shift into more occult involvement I feel a shift in my casual relationships with others. My life used to be an open book, but now I have a lot of things that I don't want to talk about with others. While at work I was reading William Walter Atkinson's book Reincarnation and the Law of Karma and a senior employee, being playfully nosy, looked over my shoulder and was clearly taken aback. He seemed to think that my book was neat, but also disquieting.

This was something of a wake up call. It all the sudden seemed like quite a bad idea indeed to have my coworkers learn that I am reading Dion Fortune's The Goat Foot God or bring in a pack of tarot cards to the break room and go through them slowly as I love to do in a quiet moment. Some people would be deeply freaked out to discover that I'm a sincere student of traditional occultism. With the way that many people already look at me (like I'm something evil) I am sincerely concerned about acquiring a reputation of witchery in the small town I live in. You can well imagine what some people would like to do with their local "transgender witch" or whatnot (since some people seem to feel full license that it's okay to hate transgender people with the same vehemence that liberals hate working class white men). I feel like transgenderism is turning into the Poland of the culture wars, if you catch my drift. I recently had a nightmare about this, which is part of why I'm bringing it up.

Point being, my life has subtly shifted in the past few months and I'm in a new phase of my life. My question is, JMG, how have you navigated this awkward phase where no one in my life is into occultism but I feel that it is prudent to keep my studies and practices under wraps, at least for now. Do you have any advice on how to navigate this sort of thing? I don't like the feeling of retreating from everyone and becoming increasingly reticent, and living in my hometown has a lot of practical advantages. Of course I have to make my own choices, but your thoughts on the matter are welcome and appreciated!

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content:
This is something that everyone goes through, and it's an important part of the work. Secrecy in occultism isn't just protective -- it's also a potent tool for becoming aware of yourself, and the way you relate to others. Yes, it has its awkward moments -- there's a reason why Manly P. Hall titled one of his books on occultism The Way of the Lonely Ones -- but on the far side of the loneliness is a capacity to relate to others in a different and far more conscious way. Being needy isn't necessarily a good thing.

One thing that works for a lot of people -- and it's something that's actively taught in some schools -- is learning to compartmentalize the occult side of your life. While there's a lot that's repellent in pop culture, there are certainly things you can do with other people that will be mutually enjoyable, and learning to accept those and enjoy them for what they are is a useful skill. I understand the desire to talk about occultism with others, but here again, there's a good reason why the fourth virtue of the mage is to be silent.

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content:
Violet's comment and your answer to Violet does seem to me relevant resp. good advice not only for occultists, but also for people who for other causes don't fit in well with society.

Booklover

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content: One of the principal places I learned how to deal with that sort of thing was in fraternal orders. If you belong to an old-fashioned lodge, you always live in two worlds at once: there's the world you share with other members of that lodge and order, and there's the world you share with everyone else, and your oaths of secrecy require you to keep the partition in place. It's an extraordinarily effective way to wake up out of the shallow trance that passes for ordinary waking consciousness, and live deliberately and intentionally.

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content:
This strikes me as something I have always known yet never thought about! Initiated into Job's Daughters just days after I turned eleven, growing up home schooled in the eighties and nineties, that makes a good deal of sense.

So everyone else doesn't manage multiple worlds on a daily basis? Hmm. I will have to think on this, particularly as regards certain aquaintances who seem to have no private life and call letting everyone know what ought to be private matters "authenticity".

BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content: In my experience, anybody who's been initiated into a fraternal order or the equivalent understands this instantly -- one more reason to encourage lodge membership! ;-)

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content: Violet, I have been feeling a bit of this as well, although not so much loneliness as feeling as if I am lacking integrity--an "in the closet" feeling, and the accompanying worry that I am somehow compromising myself by not being open and honest. Secrecy usually connotes shame or cowardice to me, so it was interesting to read JMG's more positive take on it in reply to you. Clearly I have some lessons to learn in this area. I hope you find a good balance as well!

replies.title: Re: Navigating social relationships and occult studies
replies.content: Thanks Jen! Currently about 1/3 of my secrecy is tied up in fear, 1/3 is because there simply aren't words and 1/3 is because, as JMG details in some of his occult writings, I use secrecy intentionally to produce a psychic tension that creates more consciousness in living. Certainly though, I relate heavily with the "in the closet feeling". The reality, though, is that I will do my utmost to remain a closeted student of occultism to my casual friends and associates. On an ethical level I feel, very strongly, that I have the right to secrecy and an equal right to the richness of inner world that secrecy allows. But on the matter I will say no more ;-)

Would appreciate hearing your thoughts on products and the effect, if any, of the egregors of the establishments that sell them.

OtterGirl

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Any object takes on energy from those who handle it. One of the many reasons I don't shop at Amazon is the sweatshop conditions inflicted on the people who pack and ship the orders there; that leaves its mark.

comments.title: Folk Magic Books
comments.content:
JMG – on occasion you have referred to “folk magic” and have sometimes referred to a book “The Long Lost Friend” (if I recall correctly), but I don’t recall you discussing folk magic at length or providing a “reading list”. Would it be possible for you to recommend a few of the best folk magic books that you have read (ideally, those which are still in print)?

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Folk Magic Books
replies.content: The Long Lost Friend is in print, in a very solid new edition by Daniel Harms; it's mostly of historical interest, though. In terms of practical handbooks, I favor Orion Foxwood's The Candle and the Crossroads, and the writings of Catherine Yronwode, who was my teacher in conjure: the place to start with her work is The Black Notebook, which is a collection of essays by her and others, and her online (and as yet incomplete) book on hoodoo theory and practice, which can be read here.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: When I first started reading your work I was kind of bowled over by all the things you believe - gods, magic, divination, astrology, alchemy. Are there spiritual or magical beliefs you think are wrong?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
There's that funny word, "believe." I don't find beliefs useful. I have no idea if the gods are real -- and of course that little sentence "the gods are real" embodies vast ambiguities, depending on what you mean by "real". What I know, because I've experienced it, is that if you invoke them you get certain results.

That's all we actually know about anything, you know. Gravity, for example, may or may not exist; we have literally no way to know for certain -- but we do know that if you pick up a rock and drop it, it falls, and we know that the mathematical models crafted by Newton do a great job of predicting the way things fall when dropped. Does that mean that gravity is "real"? Should we all believe in gravity? That seems silly to me. The theory of gravity is a tool, not a truth; it's useful, and that's its justification.

Are there spiritual or magical teachings and practices that aren't useful -- that do a really poor job of helping us understand the universe and live our lives? You bet. Do you remember all the yelling a few years back about how the world was going to end on December 21, 2012? That was nonsense, and I wrote a book titled Apocalpyse Not explaining why it was nonsense. There are plenty of other beliefs, especially in pop culture, that are equally nonsense -- because when you put them to the test, they fail. (The world didn't end on December 21, 2012, full stop, end of sentence.) But many other teachings and practices that are labeled "spiritual" and "magical" (two more slippery words) deliver the results they promise, and anyone who approaches them with an open mind will discover that through personal experience. That, not "belief," is what matters.

comments.title: Visualization
comments.content:
I have for about five months now made a daily practice of relaxation, meditation, divination and reflect as per Learning Ritual Magic. For the most part it has been an awesome experience, and before I get to my question, I'd like to say thank you for writing the book, it's helped me with very practical things that other texts (like Regardie's) never did.

Anyway, the one thing that has jumped out at me over the course of my practice is that I have a hard time with visualization, which came as something of a surprise to me because I used to be really good at visualizing things. All through childhood and well into my early thirties I would fall asleep every night to a parade of very detailed, often unbidden, visualizations happening across my closed eyelids. Now it's all I can do to get the vague image of light at each point in the Qabalistic Cross.

So my question is, I've seen you suggest praciticing visualization 10 minutes a day, but how exactly? Is there any specific technique you know of to improve this skill?

And, unrelated, but I thought you might get a chuckle out of this (assuming you still find the hubris of science entertaining):

http://www.tfp.org/medieval-antibiotics-offer-effective-medical-solutions/

replies.title: Re: Visualization
replies.content: That's a very common experience. Your subconscious mind is grumbling and being annoyed about being expected to submit to a little healthy discipline. It'll get over it. If you want to practice visualization, simply sit down, choose some image to visualize, and put ten minutes into imagining it as clearly as possible, using all the imaginary senses -- imagine yourself tapping on it and hearing the sound it makes, imagine the scent and texture, and so on. Over time, that'll help you pick up facility with visualization.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
My family, and my husband's family, both have a tendancy to dream the futures. The futures I dream can be changed. Fortunately, as some are horrible. My husband doesn't like to discuss it at all-but my mother-in-law (overseas) and I talked about it a couple times.

It's hereditary. I get it from my dad, who leans towards material athiesm otherwise and finds it all rather horrifying.

I've worked very slowly through much of Gareth Knight's Experience of the Inner Worlds over thevlast several years. It hasn't had any effect on the dreams one way or another.

The kids know that we don't talk about this sort of dream outside of the family. Is there anything else that is advisable to teach them about this? Girls are three and six, boys are nine, eleven, fourteen, and fifteen.

Thanks,
BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: As long as they know that it's okay, there's nothing wrong with them, the futures they dream can be changed, and you don't talk about it with strangers, they should be fine.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In one of your Galabes posts you said astrology is one way of using natural cycles for divination. What are the others?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: There are quite a few of them -- most esoteric traditions have at least one. The planetary days and hours are a system that got a lot of use in the Western esoteric tradition. The tattwas are another, which got picked up from Theosophical literature and used in the Golden Dawn. Then there's the Nine Star Ki, which has Chinese roots but is much practiced in Japan these days. Look up any of these on your favorite search engine and you'll find plenty.

comments.title: Healing Magic
comments.content:
Greetings, most venerable Archdruid, may your beard grow ever longer.

I was wondering if you could recommend a system of energy healing. I am interested in energy healing as it seems to be a very practical application of magic and one that is going to be in high demand in the coming age of economic contraction. But I don't really know where to begin. Is there a complete energy healing system that is compatible with practices described in your Druid Magic Handbook?

P.S. I am reading the Celtic Golden Dawn as well and I did like the healing system based on using the properties of herbs and foods matched against diseases.

replies.title: Re: Healing Magic
replies.content:
There is such a system, and it's included in the reconstructed early 20th century Druid correspondence course, the Dolmen Arch course, which will be published in the next year or so by Azoth Press (the same press that did my translation of Bruno's On the Shadows of the Ideas.

The system of herbal healing covered in The Celtic Golden Dawn is classic medieval Western healing lore, in the form passed down by the medical school of Myddfai in Wales. One of the books I'm considering writing down the road a bit is a book of Druid herbalism based on that tradition, and designed to work with The Celtic Golden Dawn -- but we'll see.

comments.title: meditating practice
comments.content:
Archdruid,

In martial arts training there's a problem that all students run into where they have to be proficient at multiple patterns of attack, uses of muscles, and breathing techniques. It's really easy for a student to get overwhelmed because there are just so many things to practice. The solution is to break down the art and focus on specific parts during specific times.

Since I started learning from you I've noticed just the panic inducing variety of meditation practices there are. Color meditation, discursive, empty mind, and etc...is it wise for a student to practice each of these like a martial artist practices. Do one on one day, and another on a different day?

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: meditating practice
replies.content:
The usual approach is to add things one layer at a time. First learn to relax in the posture, and take a week or two to get comfortable at it; then add the breathing, and take as much time as you need to get comfortable at that; then begin the discursive work, in a simple form, and do that for a month or more until it starts feeling natural; then add in color breathing, or what have you. The basic rule is that you're only doing one unfamiliar thing at a time.

It also helps to have a specific course to follow, as set out in a book or the lessons of an organization. Those are usually structured in the way I've just laid out, and that step-at-a-time process makes life much easier for the student.

replies.title: Re: meditating practice
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG, you have written that, astrologically speaking, one's rising sign in this life was one's sun sign in that person's previous life. When one knows one's sun sign in the present life, than, accordingly, the present sun sign should be the rising sign of one's future life. So, it should be possible to calculate in which month one will be born in one's future life. Is this correct or nonsense?

Booklover

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Knowing your future rising sign doesn't tell you which month you'll be born in, because the earth turns on its axis every 24 hours, and the rising sign is determined by the earth's rotation. Say your sun is in Leo in this life; every single day, there's a roughly two-hour window when Leo is rising, and it rotates around the cycle of the day as the sun moves from sign to sign.

The website seems to have a ton of information but I haven't found my way to a good place to start there. The rest of the internet is overwhelming. I wouldn't mind a print book or workbook if someone can recommend a good one.

Jonathan.

replies.title: Re: Resources for natal chart?
replies.content: The book I found most useful when I was first learning astrology was How to Interpret a Birth Chart by Martin Freeman; I have no idea if it's still in print, but even if it isn't, it ought to be available on the used book market. There are probably plenty of other good books on the subject now -- if anyone else has one to recommend, have at it.

comments.title: Elemental Cross
comments.content:
Hello,

I started doing the SOP a few weeks ago but have only made it as far as the elemental cross. The first day I did it, I could barely finish just that part and walked away feeling...disgust. I didn't know why I was feeling that.

Later, an idea popped into my head that had never before, that a negative thought pattern I've had for years and tried to get rid of wasn't actually a negative thought but a demon.

That night, the idea popped into my head to call on one of the angels I knew (and hadn't thought of in 30 years) from catholic school to get rid of the demon. I prayed to that angel to get rid of it and it went away. Just like that.

It's been a few weeks and I haven't had the negative thought pattern since. Gone...like magic. :) Needless to say, I've been doing the elemental cross everyday since and am working up to the rest.

1-Is the ritual really that powerful that it can have such an immediate and profound effect?
2-Did I exorcise myself? I have no reference point for this other than the movie. My head did not spin and I did not projectile vomit. It was simple, just a prayer.
3-I would like to keep this result. Is there anything I should read about, learn or do in addition to maintaining progress on the SOP.

Thank you very much! I appreciate the learning environment here.

replies.title: Re: Elemental Cross
replies.content: Yes, yes, and yes. You might consider praying to that angel more regularly; there's a rich tradition of angel magic, and you can (for example) invoke angels at the six points of the Sphere of Protection if you like. Other than that, regular practice of the SoP ought to do the trick.

comments.title: Dolmen Arch Hiatus Quandary
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

As I wrap up the fourth grade of the Dolmen Arch, I find myself in a now-familiar situation. As I've mentioned, this grade went by far better (and faster) than any of the other grades. Even so, my usual end-of-a-grade hiatus took itself right on cue. Through my work with the Dolmen Arch, I've come to understand this break-taking pretty well, and want to run that understanding by you to get your take on it generally, to ask how much you think I'm fooling myself, and to ask what advice you might have.

After careful self-reflection, my best estimate is that the breaks are driven by three factors:

1) the watcher at the threshold
2) a period of rest to assimilate the results of the previous grade's work
3) what a few weeks ago on Magic Monday you termed “karmic culmination”

The karmic culmination (if I'm using the term correctly) seems the most significant, and has taken the form of repressed emotional muck getting dredged up. It has felt each time that working through this muck has been the price for continuing on.

It seems to me that of the three factors, the watcher at the threshold plays the least part (though of course this is where I fear I might be fooling myself).

The breaks have always occurred just before completing a grade (the two most significant breaks occurred after beginning work on the grade's examination but before completing it).

The breaks invariably bear valuable fruit (via the associated muck-sorting), but also reliably run past their pull date.

Two things worth noting:

1) I have had a life-long fear of success
2) From age two to age seven I had a crippling fear of passing through thresholds (that is, literal doorways)

So again, I'm wondering what your take on the pattern and my assessment of it is, how much you think I'm fooling myself, and what advice you might have.

Thank you,

Alexander

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch Hiatus Quandary
replies.content:
It sounds to me as though you've got a very good handle on what's going on. The fact that it usually takes the form of dredging through emotional muck is a good sign that karmic culmination is involved.

I also used to have a real problem with fear of success, though I pushed my way through it -- obviously! As long as you keep going, you should be fine.

comments.title: Music and the Elemental Psyche
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

Thank you for your writings, both online and book form. The effects, first of study, then of practice in my life have been profound. My partner has also been wildly inspired by your translation of Bruno that he received a couple of months back!

I'm currently working through the Ovate grade of The Celtic Golden Dawn, and have arrived at a consistency in my daily practices. I recently read Music and the Elemental Psyche by RJ Stewart, and am delightfully intrigued (by the parts of it that I seemed to comprehend).

Would you see any conflict with working through the practices in his book alongside the Celtic Golden Dawn studies?

replies.title: Re: Music and the Elemental Psyche
replies.content:
I don't recommend mixing the two. Stewart's system is its own thing; it's a complete and powerful system, well integrated, which appears in various books of his in various forms; I worked with one version of it, the one developed in his books on Merlin, back when I was exploring a range of different systems; and I wouldn't risk mixing it with another system.

What I'd recommend instead is for you to finish your work with the Celtic Golden Dawn system, and then go on to work with Stewart's system. Once you finish a course of study in one system, it's a good idea to go on to at least one other system and do that from front to back as well. In the meantime, you can certainly collect and read Stewart's books, so you're prepared to hit the ground running once you've finished your Druid Grade work and are ready to go on to something new.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear JMG
are you by any chance familiar with the Wiccan art of knot magic? It's basically a talisman/ amulet made with a knotted rope.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. If it's Wiccan, you can pretty much assume that I don't know a thing about it; that's a tradition that has never appealed to me, and so I've left it to those who feel called to it.

comments.title: honey spells and influencing others
comments.content:
JMG,

A couple weeks ago you suggested a honey jar spell for someone who wanted to get along better with their neighbor.

I was confused, though, because this spell was trying to influence someone else's will (to become more neighborly). I heard somewhere along the line (and I'm open to changing my views, of course) that trying to interfere with someone else's will is "black magic." Of course, trying to foster positive, neighborly relations is a worthy intention, but I still was wondering about when you think it is ok to influence the will of another. I understand that a divination would certainly be part of any discernment process.

I ask this because there is a co-worker I've worked with for 10 years and with whom I will likely work with for many years to come due to the nature of our employment. To make a very long story short, I find her unfriendly and unpleasant to be around and we've clashed over several things over the years.

I have and am trying to do my inner work about all this, to look at my side of the street in the situation, yet I still find her vibe and behavior comments caustic and negative.

I would do a divination, of course, but do you think it might be a good idea to do a honey spell in which my intent is that interactions between us are more friendly? I do not wish her ill. I simply want to to enjoy work more and not have so much tension between us. For reasons I won't go into, speaking with her directly at this time doesn't appeal to me.

I would appreciate your thoughts.

Thanks,

Jacques

replies.title: Re: honey spells and influencing others
replies.content:
That's a valid and important question. To be an ethical working, a sweetening spell needs to focus on the relationship, not the person -- it's not about changing the other person's will, it's about adjusting the relationship between two people, and if you phrase the charm paper intelligently, you'll use something like "let there be peace between us."

It's important to remember that every quarrel has two sides, and there may be things you're not in the least conscious of doing that are helping to feed the unpleasantness. Very often, what happens with a sweetening spell is that those unconscious actions on your part stop, and are replaced with equally unconscious actions that encourage friendly interactions.

replies.title: Re: honey spells and influencing others
replies.content:
Very helpful. Thanks much!

Jacques

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello,

Happy Monday! Earlier you mentioned that a baby gets their own independent energy body when they are born. 15 million babies are born premature every year, do you think being born early changes their energy body in some way? If so, can the changes be fixed?

Thank you for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Premature, post-due date, right on time, it doesn't matter. Once quickening happens, the energy body takes shape as a subsystem of the mother's energy body, and once the baby starts life outside its mother's body, its energy body crystallizes into a complete auric system and takes on the astrological imprint. So there's nothing to be fixed; nature takes care of it.

comments.title: Two questions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
First, I seem to recall that in a reply to John Roth, you said that occultists tend to be leery of channeled teachings, because they don't know who is doing the talking.
More recently, I began reading "The Cosmic Doctrine" in preparation for the book club. In her introduction, Dion says that the book seems to have come from another entity speaking through her, to be transcribed by an assistant.
Is there a distinction that I'm missing?

Second, when doing a banishing, how important is it to specify the location in which the banishing takes place? I was doing a healing working for a friend this past weekend, and I phrased the banishing portion of my working as "I banish every influence that might hinder her full recovery" - with no specification of her person or surroundings.
Is this a big goof-up, or will it just lessen the effect of the working?

Thanks,
Cliff
Cliff

replies.title: Re: Two questions
replies.content:
Yes, occultists tend to be leery of channeled materials, but the reasons are rather more complex than the one you've cited, and there are modes of inner plane communication that occultists do use from time to time. "Leery" doesn't mean we have an absolute taboo about the subject, just that we don't take channeled communications on faith; before something like that is taken seriously, it's systematically tested to see whether it works or not. You may recall that John Roth expressed an equal skepticism of the run-of-the-mill channeled stuff!

As for banishing, if you had a clear intent toward the person in question, you should be fine. Place isn't necessarily that important in magic.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Good evening,
I have been stricken lately by how much big TV series have been pushed by the entertainment industry.
What is weird is that now stories are being told over the span of 24 hours of a series. IN the 80-90s, the Hollywood movies were usually around 100-110 minutes long, now this has moved to 110-120 minutes as a minimum.
Somehow it seems as if the anglo-saxon industrial culture requires ever more of people's time... It feels slightly vampiric.
Aside from that, by developing my modest capacities to remember my dreams, I have observed countless times that certain forms of modern media having taken a strong focus of mine (video games) somehow came back many years later in my dreams (even without so much as a story in the dream). And I don't really consider myself to have consumed that much of mass media, video games or anything else.
It leads me to think that perhaps our minds do need to digest the content they absorb and play with it further than just absorbing it passively.

My question would be, what are the effects on the rich world of the suppression of that "digestion" process ? One naive answer would be that we just become less and less able to think correctly, in the same way as an overloaded digestive system is no longer able to healthily process food.
Or it would just tend to make us more shallow, because we would be always pressured to cram more timelength of contents into ever more packed lives...


However, given my intuitive understanding of the laws introduced earlier on the main blog, I suspect that the "processing" activity being suppressed is somehow coming out in a hidden form. Just like overheating would cause you to vomit... it might come out as temporary bouts of collective madness (a psychological answer). However, what about an occult answer ? What kind of non-physical living being might it feed ?

If we no longer collectively process certain aspects of what makes us collectively human, what spiritual phenomena are we processing instead, in the background? I don't suppose it would look like an old reassuring wise grandma...

Jean-Vivien

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Basically, when mass culture suppresses the process of reflection and "digestion" of mental imagery and input, you can expect to see a broad general movement toward mindlessness -- in place of reasoned discussion and independent thought, automatic repetition of sound bites; in place of empathy and openness to interaction, rigid emotional patterns producing explosive outbursts of rage, hatred, and terror. I don't know that it feeds any of the various nonphysical beings, but it certainly provides a congenial atmosphere for those potent, primitive, and unbalanced beings that magical tradition calls "demons."

There's a great scene in Marlowe's play Doctor Faustus where Faustus challenges Mephistopheles, demanding to know how the demon got out of Hell to show up in Faustus' living room. "Why, this is Hell," says Mephistopheles, "nor am I ever out of it." Marlowe knew his magical philosophy, and understood that summoning demons is the easiest thing in the world; all you have to do is descend to their level, and there they are...

comments.title: Green Ray
comments.content: I have a book in which Dion Fortune says somebody was messing with the Green Ray entirely too much for Dion’s peace of mind.  Googling yielded mixed results.  What exactly was the Green Ray and why did she think it was a big no-no?

replies.title: Re: Green Ray
replies.content: The theory of the Rays is one of the things that Theosophy introduced to occultism: a useful system of symbolism keyed to the seven colors of the spectrum. The Green Ray corresponds to Venus among the planets, and it's the Ray of the forces of nature, including but not limited to those expressed in sexuality. Fortune didn't think the Green Ray was a no-no -- she used it herself extensively in her workings. The problem, as she saw it, was that too much of any one Ray tends to land you in various kinds of imbalance, and the Green Ray in particular could be extremely intoxicating and dangerous for mental health if overindulged in by someone still reacting against Britain's state of Victorian spiritual constipation.

comments.title: druid magic
comments.content:
Hi JM, thanks for providing this venue for discussion.

I have a few brief questions and while I know what I shoiuld expect ("experiment and find what works for you") I thought I would air them anyhow. So here goes!

1. I have been practicing from the Druid Magic, book since new years. I've clocked over 100 days with a few missed for a variety of reasons, but as I've got closer to completing the sphere of protection, to the end of chapter 4, I've found it increasingly likely I won't get consecutive days done. Hangovers are one reason, also family & work, comittments - another is illness. So what's weird is a few times I've missed a practice and I get sick the next day - nothing serious, but a cold/flu like symptoms deal, which then keeps me for another couple of days. Maybe it's just a data point or two but I found it curious enough to mention.

2. Trouble with meditation. I can normally do the 10 mins of relaxing and square breathing, but then find it difficult to do the actual meditation. I know what to do and what it takes but as you mention above to Jenn (I think), it's hard work.

3. I believe you mentioned this before, but I do the divination with Ogham cards in the same space I perform the SoP and med, normally at the end. I think I remember you saying it should be done in a different space - can you clarify, please, if this is the case?

I can feel the benefits of the work and feel real world changes, though subtle. I appreciate your efforts and enjoy and leaving more about who I am and what is possible. Thank you

replies.title: Re: druid magic
replies.content:
1 and 2 are versions of the same thing, and get the same answer. Yes, it's hard work -- it's extremely hard work, and one of the things that makes it hard is that your subconscious mind will kick like an irate mule against the discipline. There's no easy solution; all you can do is keep on keeping on, with the knowledge that eventually it becomes a lot easier.

This is really the most important test of your fitness for initiation. If you have the stubbornness to just keep slogging away despite it all, you pass the test and the door opens. If you don't, the door stays shut. You can always return and try again, but until you set aside everything else and just keep on doing the work, it will stay closed.

As for your Ogham practice, if it works for you to cast the reading in a banished space, by all means keep doing that.

replies.title: Re: druid magic
replies.content:
Thank you for your responses. One last thing - what circumstances do you recommend missing a practice. Like, is being in a bit of a hangover, or low key virus a good reason to avoid it in the same way you would say not to practice under the influence of certain drugs/alcohol?

Or is it ok to do under all occasions?

replies.title: Re: druid magic
replies.content: I don't recommend skipping practices for any reason short of complete physical incapacity. (The last time I skipped a practice, which was quite a few years ago, I was really sick, and slept straight through most of a day.) Even if the practice is just a matter of fumbling through forms by rote, the effort of will needed to do it is excellent training and will pay off.

replies.title: Re: druid magic
replies.content: Thank you. That's good to know and I shall remind myself of that when the nagging procrastination appears!

replies.title: Re: druid magic
replies.content: For what it's worth, I do my divination in the same space as my SoP, and the cards seem remarkably clear, consistent, and accurate, rather to my surprise--I was expecting a lot of ambiguity and incorrect interpretations, being new at divination, but they are almost suspiciously on point. They are so firm and consistent that I actually get the feeling sometimes that if they were personified they would be sighing and saying, "We've been over this..."

replies.title: Re: druid magic
replies.content: Most interesting. Thank you for the data point!

comments.title: Avoiding inadvertent magical workings
comments.content:
I don't practice magic or carry out rituals, they are just not my thing. However I follow your other work on recommending mental training like memory work. I've skimmed The Art of Memory by Yates and done some of the modern memory training work.
I was considering using some of the constructions of sacred geometry, or the tree of life, as the basis for memory palaces - easy to visualize and internally consistent. What are your thoughts on this, could I end up in places I shouldn't be?

replies.title: Re: Avoiding inadvertent magical workings
replies.content: Nope. Tens of thousands of people in the Middle Ages and Renaissance practiced the Art of Memory using just about every framework you can imagine, and I don't know of a single reported case of accidental magic coming out of it. If you want to pursue the Art of Memory, go ye forth and do that thing!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Concerning meditation, based on your posts on the blog and having read The Druid Magic Handbook, I've decided to take on a practice of discursive meditation after having done an anapanasati (mindfulness of breath) practice for the last three years. So far, it's felt like a lurch forward - I'm no longer in a comfort zone and things don't feel as stable as they've felt lately during practice.

That said, I don't feel like I should necessarily abandon the absorptive/emptying side of things, and I was wondering if there's precedent for combining the two in western traditions like there is in Buddhism. For example, the states of meditative absorption known as the jhanas are apparently meant to be followed by meditating on things like the Four Noble Truths (or so I read in Leigh Brasington's Right Concentration).

I can see why doing the purely mind-emptying methods is problematic; I've started to see articles from people trained in Buddhist traditions saying how this co-opting of mindfulness, especially by corporations pushing it towards their employees, is problematic. It's stripped, at the very least, of other side of the coin, which would be the discursive portion on what would be the ethical and metaphysical teachings.

Best,

-Jean-Pierre.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't recommend mixing systems. If you want to do that kind of combined discursive and mind-emptying meditation, I'd strongly encourage you to find a teacher or a good instructional book on a form of Buddhist meditation that does that, and follow it to the letter.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
This may be too late, and it's actually more of a gripe than a question...

Geomancy.... This term confuses me every time. Here in Asia it means things like feng shui, connected to the geo*graphy* of the user. You seem to be using it in a different way... geo*metry*? Like... mandalas? I know you mentioned the Yi Jing (which I use a lot) as a parallel... but I still get confused. I know, I know, I could Google it, or find it on Wikipedia. It just irritates me that the same word is used for two very different practices.

That is all. Carping is complete.

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
It's not too late -- anything that comes in before midnight eastern time (GMT - 4 hours in daylight savings time, 5 hours in standard time) is fair game and will get an answer as long as it's not trolling).

Geomancy, Latin geomantia, is a very rough translation of the Arabic phrase 'ilm ar-Raml, "science of the sand," so called because in the Muslim world, early on, the most common writing surface used for it was the sands of the desert. The Latin term was in use by the 12th century CE, when the first Arabic geomantic texts were translated into Latin for Europeans to use, and the use of the same word for feng shui came much later...

comments.title: Dolmen Arch vs Druid Magic Handbook
comments.content:
Perhaps this was covered already and I just missed it but, I've seen you mention your upcoming Dolmen Arch books. How does the material relate to that in the Druid Magic Handbook? Is it a more advanced continuation of it or it's own system entirely?

I also recall you mentioning some time ago that shortly before you left your Arch-Druid position you had a disagreement with the order over the path being taken regarding future magical work, something about 'spirals', Is that some of whats in the Dolmen Arch course or again, is it something different entirely?

replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch vs Druid Magic Handbook
replies.content:
The Dolmen Arch is my reconstruction of an early 20th century American Druid Revival correspondence course. It uses the same basic practices as The Druid Magic Handbook -- the Sphere of Protection et al. -- and the same overall symbolism, but expands it in different directions; it includes a lot of philosophy and mind training, and includes a method of energy healing and a range of other interesting things. I'd say it has about a 20% overlap with The Druid Magic Handbook -- meaning that about 80% of what's in either book isn't in the other.

As for the Spirals, no, that wasn't a disagreement with the other members of the Grand Grove; I simply came to the conclusion that I'd been mistaken to replace the original scheme of seven specified optional fields of study with something more freeform. That has nothing to do with what's in the Dolmen Arch course, either.

comments.title: Beliefs, Chaos, and What is Magick exactly?
comments.content:
Hi John,

Just read your comment further down and I'm excerpting "I have no idea if the gods are real ... What I know, because I've experienced it, is that if you invoke them you get certain results."

Now, that's been my experience as well - although I usually pray more openly to "The Universe", 'Life", and sometimes "Teachers and Guides". And I usually ask for a lesson or an understanding, or sometimes just for strength, and not for anything specifically material. Do you think anthropomorphizing the deities makes them more potent?

That leads me to my second question. Have you read "The Psychonaut Field Guide" (https://bluefluke.deviantart.com/art/The-Psychonaut-Field-Manual-FOURTH-PDF-EDITION-530005584)

and what you think of it?

He says something similar to you in one of the first pages: "Belief is a tool" and goes into distinguishing deities you invoke versus evoke. Call vs create is I think his critical distinction.

Anyway, what you call magick still seems a lot like positive thinking with faith included, and I am not disparaging what you teach in saying that. I think it was Anthony Robbins who said "Some things are worth believing whether or not they are true."

Comments? And what distinguishes magick from PMA and faith and good psychology?

replies.title: Re: Beliefs, Chaos, and What is Magick exactly?
replies.content:
To my mind, it's just as unjustified to insist that we know that we create deities as it is to insist that we know that they're out there objectively. As human beings, we know squat. All we know is that certain things seem to work, and that we can come up with an infinite number of plausible theories to pretend to explain why. Thus it's as reasonable to ask why you deanthropomorphize deities as it is to ask why I anthropomorphize them; both of these are, strictly speaking, arbitrary acts based on insufficient knowledge, justified only by their apparent effects.

I don't call anything "magick," by the way -- that verbal tic of Crowley's is one that I've always found annoying. The word in our language is "magic," without a K. (I'm reminded suddenly of an old Monty Python skit; "Kopperfield with a K...")

As for what distinguishes magic from PMA (whatever that is) and faith and good psychology, two things. The first is three thousand years of so of accumulated practical experience; the second is a worldview that hasn't capitulated to the dogmatic materialism of modern culture. In my experience, those produce better results than the alternatives; besides, it's so fun to watch rationalists lose it when magic comes up in conversation!

comments.title: Natural vs ritural magic.
comments.content:
Hi JMG

What are the differences between the effects and uses of natural and ritural magic. Would they reinforce each other?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Natural vs ritual magic.
replies.content: Natural magic is subtler and less dramatic, but it's also a lot easier to use. Ritual magic demands a lot of hard work and training, but gets more dramatic results. Yes, the two are very often combined.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Not really asking a question here so much as mentioning an unfortunate discovery I made in April. Each April I get a three - four week blast of allergies, and the simplest way to manage them is claritin, or the generic equivalent. Last year, when I wasn't much practicing meditation in the spring, I would have told a person asking me about it that I preferred it over the other chemicals on the market because of a lack of side effects. This year, going into allergy season with a relatively well developed meditation habit I am sad to notice that there are major side effects! It dulls my mind vividly and untempers the will. For the three weeks I was on it my ritual and meditation pattern was erratic and when I willed to do it an abjectly dead experience. Now that I am out off the drug my previous patterns of meditation and ritual are starting to come back into shape. Maybe next week I will have combed some of my reflections on the contrast between weeks with out practice and the return to practicing.

Ray Wharton (I am testing if I like bothering to have a dreamwidth account at the moment)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Interesting! Thanks for the data point.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Claritin is known to have bad effects on concentration for some people. I think allegra is not supposed to do that; at any rate, when I was first put on claritin it had a rather dramatic impact for me, and I was switched to allegra, which did not. So, you might find an antihistamine other than claritin works better for you.
